```{r ch14_npe_training_data}
      start_idx <- (b - 1) * batch_size + 1
      end_idx   <- min(b * batch_size, n_train_sims)
      indices   <- start_idx:end_idx
      
      all_responses[[b]] <- future_map(
        indices,
        function(i) simulate_model_a(train_thetas[i, ]),
        .options = furrr_options(seed = TRUE)
      )
      gc() # Force garbage collection
    }
    
    # Restore original plan
    plan(old_plan)
  
    train_y_mat <- do.call(rbind, unlist(all_responses, recursive = FALSE))
  
    saveRDS(list(thetas = train_thetas, responses = train_y_mat),
            npe_cache_file)
    cat("Training data saved:", n_train_sims, "simulations.\n")
  } else {
    stop("Training data not found. Set run_intensive_checks = TRUE to generate it.")
  }
} else {
  cached_train <- readRDS(npe_cache_file)
  train_thetas <- cached_train$thetas
  train_y_mat  <- cached_train$responses
  n_train_sims <- nrow(train_thetas)
  cat("Loaded", n_train_sims, "cached training simulations.\n")
}
```

### NPE Prior Predictive Check

Before training the network, we check that the NPE prior — the joint Box-Uniform on $(\text{logit}\,\varepsilon,\ \log N_\text{particles},\ \text{logit}\,\mu)$ — produces a range of cumulative-accuracy curves that covers the cognitively plausible behaviour space. This is the *NPE-side* prior predictive: parameters drawn from the actual three-parameter prior used by the inference network, pushed through the actual simulator on the actual fixed schedule. It is distinct from the cross-model PPC we showed earlier (which used Model B's $\varepsilon$ prior alone with $\mu = 0$).

Three things to look for:

1. **Coverage.** The 5–95% ribbon should contain everything from chance-level (flat at 0.5) to near-perfect learners. If it does not, the prior is too tight.
2. **No degenerate concentrations.** Mass should not pile up at any one accuracy value; that would signal the simulator is ignoring some parameter.
3. **Well-behaved tails.** A small fraction of curves at chance is expected (high-$\varepsilon$, high-$\mu$ learners); a large fraction is a red flag.

Because the training dataset of size $N$ already realises this prior predictive, we can read it directly off `train_y_mat` rather than rerunning simulations.

```{r ch14_npe_prior_predictive}
n_ppc_show <- min(500, n_train_sims)
ppc_idx    <- sample.int(n_train_sims, n_ppc_show)

ppc_curves <- map_dfr(ppc_idx, function(i) {
  resp    <- train_y_mat[i, ]
  fb      <- npe_cat_one
  correct <- as.integer(resp == fb)
  tibble(
    sample_id = i,
    trial     = seq_along(resp),
    cum_acc   = cumsum(correct) / seq_along(resp)
  )
})

ppc_summary <- ppc_curves |>
  group_by(trial) |>
  summarise(
    q05 = quantile(cum_acc, 0.05), q25 = quantile(cum_acc, 0.25),
    q50 = median(cum_acc),
    q75 = quantile(cum_acc, 0.75), q95 = quantile(cum_acc, 0.95),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(ppc_summary, aes(x = trial)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = q05, ymax = q95), fill = "#56B4E9", alpha = 0.20) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = q25, ymax = q75), fill = "#56B4E9", alpha = 0.40) +
  geom_line(aes(y = q50), color = "#0072B2", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(
    title    = "NPE Prior Predictive: Cumulative Accuracy Across Training Simulations",
    subtitle = paste0("Median + 50%/90% intervals across ", n_ppc_show,
                      " parameter draws from the NPE prior."),
    x = "Trial", y = "Cumulative accuracy"
  )
```

The training dataset doubles as the prior predictive sample. If the ribbon above looks acceptable, the prior is doing its job; if it does not, fix the prior before spending compute on training.

### Setting Up the Python Environment

```{r ch14_npe_setup}
# ── Python environment ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
library(reticulate)

# Force-clear parallel plan before touching Python
if (requireNamespace("future", quietly = TRUE)) {
  future::plan(future::sequential)
}
gc()

# macOS Safety Valves - Extreme Edition
Sys.setenv(KMP_DUPLICATE_LIB_OK = "TRUE")
Sys.setenv(OMP_DUPLICATE_LIB_OK = "TRUE")
Sys.setenv(OMP_NUM_THREADS = "1")
Sys.setenv(MKL_NUM_THREADS = "1")

if (python_available) {
  torch <- import("torch", delay_load = FALSE)

  # Force single-threading in all libraries to prevent R/Python OMP conflicts.
  # MPS (Apple GPU) is used instead of multi-threading for parallelism on M1.
  torch$set_num_threads(1L)

  sbi      <- import("sbi")
  np       <- import("numpy")
  utils    <- import("sbi.utils")
  infer    <- import("sbi.inference")
  warnings <- import("warnings")
  # Suppress the constant-features warning: context_per_trial is intentionally
  # identical across all simulations (fixed trial schedule for path-dependency).
  warnings$filterwarnings("ignore", message = "Data has constant values")

  # ── M1 / Apple Silicon: use Metal Performance Shaders (MPS) backend ──────────
  # MPS exposes the M1 GPU to PyTorch and gives ~3–5x speedup over single-CPU
  # for neural spline flow training and ~2x for inference forward passes.
  # Falls back to CPU silently on non-Apple hardware or older PyTorch (<2.0).
  # PYTORCH_ENABLE_MPS_FALLBACK=1 allows ops without MPS kernels to run on CPU.
  Sys.setenv(PYTORCH_ENABLE_MPS_FALLBACK = "1")
  mps_available <- tryCatch(
    torch$backends$mps$is_available(),
    error = function(e) FALSE
  )
  npe_device <- if (mps_available) torch$device("mps") else torch$device("cpu")

  cat("sbi version:", sbi$`__version__`, "\n")
  cat("torch version:", torch$`__version__`, "\n")
  cat("Device:", if (mps_available) "MPS (Apple GPU)" else "CPU", "\n")
  cat("Threads (Torch):", as.integer(torch$get_num_threads()), "\n")
  cat("Threads (OMP):  ", Sys.getenv("OMP_NUM_THREADS"), "\n")
} else {
  warning("Python/sbi environment not found. NPE chunks will fail.")
  npe_device <- NULL
}
```

### Step 3: Training the NPE Inference Network

This is where the neural network learns the mapping from observations to posteriors. The `sbi` package handles the training loop; we need to provide three things: (1) a prior object in sbi's format, (2) the training tensors, and (3) the network architecture.

**The prior in sbi.** The `sbi` library needs an explicit prior object to define the support of $\theta$ — the range of values the network will be expected to handle. We use a `BoxUniform` prior (a box uniform distribution over a hyperrectangle), with bounds set at $\pm 3$ standard deviations for the Normal priors and at the exact log-scale endpoints for the log-uniform $N$ prior. The network will not be reliable outside these bounds, so they should be wide enough to contain all plausible parameter values.

**The network architecture.** `sbi`'s `SNPE` class builds both the embedding network and the normalising flow automatically. We specify `density_estimator = "nsf"` (neural spline flow). Internally, `sbi` constructs:
- An **embedding network**: a small MLP that takes the flattened $[y, \text{context}]$ vector (length $T \times 4$) and outputs a 64-dimensional summary vector.
- A **neural spline flow**: a sequence of coupling layers where each bijection uses rational-quadratic splines parameterised by small neural networks that condition on the embedding.

**Training.** The `inference_obj$train()` call runs mini-batch gradient descent on the loss $-\frac{1}{N}\sum_i \log q_\phi(\theta^{(i)} \mid y^{(i)})$ using the Adam optimiser. Each batch of 256 $(\theta, y)$ pairs contributes a gradient update to both the embedding network and the flow. Training typically converges within a few hundred epochs (each epoch passes through the full 20k dataset once). On a CPU this takes 5–15 minutes; on a GPU it is much faster.

**What does convergence look like?** The `show_train_summary = TRUE` flag prints the training and validation loss at each epoch. A well-trained network shows the validation loss decreasing and levelling off without diverging from the training loss. If the two diverge (overfitting), reduce the network size or increase the training data. If neither loss decreases, the learning rate may be too low.

**Saving the trained model.** We save only the `state_dict` — the learned weights — rather than the full Python object, because Python objects are not safely serialisable across sessions. Loading requires rebuilding the same network architecture and loading the weights back in; the `sbi` documentation covers the exact steps.

```{r ch14_npe_train, eval=python_available}
npe_model_file <- here("simdata", "ch14_npe_trained_posterior.pkl")

# We need the prior and inference object setup regardless of training or loading
# theta = [logit(eps), log(N_particles), logit(mu)]
prior_low  <- torch$tensor(
  c(LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_MEAN - 3 * LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_SD, log(20), LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_MEAN - 3 * LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_SD),
  dtype = torch$float32
)
prior_high <- torch$tensor(
  c(LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_MEAN + 3 * LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_SD, log(300), LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_MEAN + 3 * LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_SD),
  dtype = torch$float32
)
sbi_prior <- utils$BoxUniform(low = prior_low, high = prior_high)

inference_obj <- infer$SNPE(prior = sbi_prior, density_estimator = "nsf")

if (regenerate_simulations || !file.exists(npe_model_file)) {
  if (run_intensive_checks) {
    # Create tensors on CPU — sbi stores training data on CPU (data_device).
    # The training loop moves batches to the training device (MPS) internally.
    # Sending tensors to MPS before append_simulations() triggers a warning.
    theta_tensor <- torch$tensor(train_thetas, dtype = torch$float32)

    # Build data tensor: concatenate [response_t, height_t, position_t, feedback_t]
    # Shape: (n_sims, n_trials * 4)
    x_list <- lapply(seq_len(n_train_sims), function(i) {
      resp <- train_y_mat[i, ]
      as.numeric(t(cbind(resp, context_per_trial)))
    })
    x_mat    <- do.call(rbind, x_list)
    x_tensor <- torch$tensor(x_mat, dtype = torch$float32)

    inference_obj$append_simulations(theta_tensor, x_tensor)

    cat("Training NPE on device:", ifelse(mps_available, "MPS", "CPU"), "...\n")
    density_estimator <- inference_obj$train(
      training_batch_size = 256L,
      learning_rate       = 5e-4,
      show_train_summary  = TRUE
    )
  
    # Build the posterior object using vectorized MCMC (slice) for speed/stability
    npe_posterior <- inference_obj$build_posterior(
      density_estimator,
      sample_with = "mcmc",
      mcmc_method = "slice_np_vectorized",
      posterior_parameters = list(warmup_steps = 100L)
    )
    cat("NPE training complete.\n")
  
    # Save the trained model state
    torch$save(density_estimator$state_dict(), npe_model_file)
    cat("Trained NPE saved to", npe_model_file, "\n")
  } else {
    stop("Trained model not found. Set run_intensive_checks = TRUE to train it.")
  }
} else {
  # Load the pre-trained state dict
  density_estimator <- inference_obj$train(max_num_epochs = 0L, show_train_summary = FALSE)
  state_dict <- torch$load(npe_model_file)
  density_estimator$load_state_dict(state_dict)
  
  # Build the posterior object using vectorized MCMC (slice) for speed/stability
  npe_posterior <- inference_obj$build_posterior(
    density_estimator,
    sample_with = "mcmc",
    mcmc_method = "slice_np_vectorized",
    posterior_parameters = list(warmup_steps = 100L)
  )
  cat("Loaded pre-trained NPE model.\n")
}
```

### Step 4: Performing Inference on the Observed Data

After training, inference for any new participant is a single forward pass through the network — no MCMC, no additional simulation. This is the **amortisation** payoff: the entire computational cost was paid at training time, and inference now costs milliseconds per subject.

The observed data $y_\text{obs}$ must be formatted identically to the training data: a flattened vector of $[y_t, \text{height}_t, \text{position}_t, \text{feedback}_t]$ for all $T$ trials. The `npe_posterior$sample()` call passes this vector through the trained embedding network, obtains the 64-dimensional summary $\hat{s}(y_\text{obs})$, conditions the normalising flow on it, and draws samples from the resulting distribution over $\theta$.

The returned samples are on the unconstrained (logit / log) scales on which the network was trained. We back-transform with `plogis()` and `exp()` to recover $\varepsilon$, $\mu$, and $N_\text{particles}$ on their natural scales. These 10,000 posterior samples can be treated exactly like MCMC draws: compute means, credible intervals, posterior predictive distributions, or LOO weights from them.

**What to look for.** A well-calibrated NPE posterior for $\varepsilon$ should be centred near the true generating value (0.1 in our simulated example). If the posterior is much wider than the prior, the data are uninformative and the model is not identified for this dataset — expected for short sequences. If it is sharply narrower than the prior, the response sequence contains strong information about the error rate. The posterior for $\mu$ is expected to be wider on static data because a static task cannot distinguish a low-mutation learner from a zero-mutation learner — both will stop replacing hypotheses once the correct rule is found.

```{r ch14_npe_inference, eval=python_available}
# Build the observed data tensor (same format as training)
obs_responses <- as.integer(fit_sim$sim_response)
obs_x_vec     <- as.numeric(t(cbind(obs_responses, context_per_trial)))
obs_x_mat     <- matrix(obs_x_vec, nrow = 1)
obs_x_tensor  <- torch$tensor(obs_x_mat, dtype = torch$float32)

# Sample from the NPE posterior
n_npe_samples <- 2000L
npe_samples   <- npe_posterior$sample(
  sample_shape       = reticulate::tuple(n_npe_samples),
  x                  = obs_x_tensor,
  show_progress_bars = TRUE
)
npe_draws <- as.matrix(npe_samples$numpy())
colnames(npe_draws) <- c("logit_eps", "log_n_particles", "logit_mu")

npe_eps    <- plogis(npe_draws[, "logit_eps"])
npe_n_part <- exp(npe_draws[, "log_n_particles"])
npe_mu     <- plogis(npe_draws[, "logit_mu"])

cat("NPE posterior for ε:           mean =", round(mean(npe_eps), 4),
    "  95% CI = [", round(quantile(npe_eps, 0.025), 4), ",",
    round(quantile(npe_eps, 0.975), 4), "]\n")
cat("NPE posterior for N_particles: mean =", round(mean(npe_n_part), 1),
    "  95% CI = [", round(quantile(npe_n_part, 0.025), 1), ",",
    round(quantile(npe_n_part, 0.975), 1), "]\n")
cat("NPE posterior for μ:           mean =", round(mean(npe_mu), 4),
    "  95% CI = [", round(quantile(npe_mu, 0.025), 4), ",",
    round(quantile(npe_mu, 0.975), 4), "]\n")

p_eps <- ggplot(tibble(eps = npe_eps), aes(x = eps)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)),
                 fill = "#009E73", alpha = 0.7, bins = 40) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.1, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "NPE Posterior for ε (Model A)",
       subtitle = "Red dashed: true generating ε = 0.1",
       x = "ε", y = "Density")

p_npart <- ggplot(tibble(n_part = npe_n_part), aes(x = n_part)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)),
                 fill = "#0072B2", alpha = 0.7, bins = 40) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 100, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "NPE Posterior for N_particles (Model A)",
       subtitle = "Red dashed: true generating N_particles = 100",
       x = "N_particles", y = "Density")

# The μ posterior is expected to be largely prior-like on static data:
# the Kruschke task gives the filter no reason to mutate rapidly, so the
# data are uninformative about mutation rate. On contingent-shift or drift
# data the posterior should sharpen. The prior overlay makes this visible.
prior_mu_draws <- plogis(rnorm(n_npe_samples, LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_MEAN, LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_SD))
p_mu <- ggplot() +
  geom_density(aes(x = prior_mu_draws), fill = "grey70", alpha = 0.5,
               linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_histogram(aes(x = npe_mu, y = after_stat(density)),
                 fill = "#CC79A7", alpha = 0.7, bins = 40) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.0, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "NPE Posterior for μ (Model A)",
       subtitle = "Grey = prior. Red dashed: true generating μ = 0.0 (static task).\nPosterior ≈ prior signals μ is not identified on static data.",
       x = "μ", y = "Density")

print(p_eps / p_npart / p_mu)
```

**Reading the NPE posterior.** Three things to check: (1) the posteriors for $\varepsilon$ and $N_{\text{particles}}$ should contain the true generating values; (2) $N_{\text{particles}}$ should show a meaningful posterior — if it is prior-like, the data are uninformative about working-memory capacity for this task length; (3) the posterior for $\mu$ is **expected to be largely prior-like on static data** — the Kruschke task provides no signal about mutation rate because the correct rule does not change. This is the right result, not a failure. The prior overlay in the $\mu$ panel makes the information gain (or lack thereof) visible. A narrow $\mu$ posterior on non-stationary data would be the substantive finding.

### NPE Posterior Predictive Check

Having drawn samples from $q_\phi(\theta \mid y_\text{obs})$, we check that the posterior actually predicts the observed data. We push posterior $\theta$ draws back through the simulator and compare the resulting cumulative-accuracy curves against the observed agent's curve. Two failure modes to watch for: (i) the observed curve falls outside the posterior predictive ribbon — sign of model misspecification or NPE under-coverage; (ii) the ribbon is much wider than the observed variability — sign that the network has not extracted enough information from the data.

```{r ch14_npe_posterior_predictive, eval=python_available}
n_ppc_post <- 200
ppc_idx_post <- sample.int(nrow(npe_draws), n_ppc_post)

ppc_post_curves <- map_dfr(ppc_idx_post, function(i) {
  theta_i <- npe_draws[i, ]
  resp    <- simulate_model_a(theta_i)
  correct <- as.integer(resp == npe_cat_one)
  tibble(
    sample_id = i,
    trial     = seq_along(resp),
    cum_acc   = cumsum(correct) / seq_along(resp)
  )
})

ppc_post_summary <- ppc_post_curves |>
  group_by(trial) |>
  summarise(
    q05 = quantile(cum_acc, 0.05), q25 = quantile(cum_acc, 0.25),
    q50 = median(cum_acc),
    q75 = quantile(cum_acc, 0.75), q95 = quantile(cum_acc, 0.95),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

obs_curve <- tibble(
  trial   = seq_along(obs_responses),
  cum_acc = cumsum(as.integer(obs_responses == npe_cat_one)) /
            seq_along(obs_responses)
)

ggplot(ppc_post_summary, aes(x = trial)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = q05, ymax = q95), fill = "#009E73", alpha = 0.20) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = q25, ymax = q75), fill = "#009E73", alpha = 0.40) +
  geom_line(aes(y = q50), color = "#006D4E", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(data = obs_curve, aes(x = trial, y = cum_acc),
            color = "red", linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(
    title    = "NPE Posterior Predictive Check (Model A)",
    subtitle = "Red = observed cumulative accuracy. Green ribbons = 50% / 90% posterior predictive.",
    x = "Trial", y = "Cumulative accuracy"
  )
```

The observed curve should lie inside the 90% ribbon for almost all trials; isolated escapes are tolerable, sustained departures are not. Unlike the cross-model PPC we showed for Model B (which used Model A's simulator with Model B's prior), this check uses the actual NPE posterior on the actual NPE simulator — it is the within-model PPC the Model B section could not produce.

### NPE Validation: SBC + Expected Coverage (Phase 6)

We need to perform calibration checks (see @sec-model-quality-checks). For NPE, the standard diagnostics are marginal SBC (Talts et al. 2018) and expected coverage (Hermans et al. 2022). Because NPE is amortized, running 1000 calibration checks takes seconds — each requires only a forward pass through the trained network.

```{r ch14_npe_validation, eval=run_intensive_checks}
n_cal <- 1000L

npe_val_file <- here("simdata", "ch14_npe_validation_results.rds")

if (regenerate_simulations || !file.exists(npe_val_file)) {
  cat("Running NPE calibration diagnostics (", as.integer(n_cal), " checks)...\n")

  # Generate calibration set: draw theta from prior, simulate x.
  # Three parameters: logit(eps), log(N_particles), logit(mu).
  set.seed(7777)
  cal_logit_eps <- rnorm(n_cal, LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_MEAN, LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_SD)
  cal_log_npart <- runif(n_cal, log(20), log(300))
  cal_logit_mu  <- rnorm(n_cal, LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_MEAN,  LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_SD)
  cal_thetas    <- cbind(cal_logit_eps, cal_log_npart, cal_logit_mu)

  # Generate 1000 calibration sets sequentially to avoid crashes
  cal_responses <- lapply(
    seq_len(n_cal),
    function(i) simulate_model_a(cal_thetas[i, ])
  )
  cal_y_mat <- do.call(rbind, cal_responses)

  # Build calibration data tensors
  cal_x_list <- lapply(seq_len(n_cal), function(i) {
    resp <- cal_y_mat[i, ]
    as.numeric(t(cbind(resp, context_per_trial)))
  })
  cal_x_mat    <- do.call(rbind, cal_x_list)
  cal_x_tensor <- torch$tensor(cal_x_mat, dtype = torch$float32)

  # ── Marginal SBC ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  n_post_samples <- 1000L
  sbc_ranks <- matrix(0L, nrow = n_cal, ncol = 3)
  colnames(sbc_ranks) <- c("logit_eps", "log_n_particles", "logit_mu")

  for (i in seq_len(n_cal)) {
    post_samples <- npe_posterior$sample(
      sample_shape = reticulate::tuple(n_post_samples),
      x            = cal_x_tensor[i - 1L]$unsqueeze(0L)
    )$numpy()

    for (j in 1:3) {
      sbc_ranks[i, j] <- sum(post_samples[, j] < cal_thetas[i, j])
    }
  }

  sbc_df <- as_tibble(sbc_ranks) |>
    pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "parameter", values_to = "rank")

  # ── Expected Coverage ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  coverage_levels <- seq(0.05, 0.95, by = 0.05)
  empirical_coverage <- vapply(coverage_levels, function(alpha) {
    covered <- vapply(seq_len(n_cal), function(i) {
      post_samples <- npe_posterior$sample(
        sample_shape = reticulate::tuple(n_post_samples),
        x            = cal_x_tensor[i - 1L]$unsqueeze(0L)
      )$numpy()
      q_lo <- quantile(post_samples[, 1], (1 - alpha) / 2)
      q_hi <- quantile(post_samples[, 1], 1 - (1 - alpha) / 2)
      cal_thetas[i, 1] >= q_lo & cal_thetas[i, 1] <= q_hi
    }, logical(1))
    mean(covered)
  }, numeric(1))

  coverage_df <- tibble(nominal = coverage_levels, empirical = empirical_coverage)
  
  saveRDS(list(sbc_df = sbc_df, coverage_df = coverage_df), npe_val_file)
} else {
  npe_val_res <- readRDS(npe_val_file)
  sbc_df <- npe_val_res$sbc_df
  coverage_df <- npe_val_res$coverage_df
}

p_sbc <- ggplot(sbc_df, aes(x = rank)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 20,
                 fill = "#009E73", alpha = 0.7) +
  facet_wrap(~parameter, scales = "free_x") +
  labs(title = "Marginal SBC Rank Histograms (NPE, Model A)",
       subtitle = "Uniform = calibrated. U-shape = overconfident. Hump = underconfident.",
       x = "Rank", y = "Count")

p_coverage <- ggplot(coverage_df, aes(x = nominal, y = empirical)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = nominal - 2 * sqrt(nominal * (1 - nominal) / n_cal),
                  ymax = nominal + 2 * sqrt(nominal * (1 - nominal) / n_cal)),
              fill = "grey80", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_line(color = "#0072B2", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "#0072B2", size = 2) +
  labs(title = "Expected Coverage (NPE, Model A)",
       subtitle = "Points on diagonal = well-calibrated. Grey band = 2SE.",
       x = "Nominal coverage", y = "Empirical coverage") +
  coord_equal(xlim = c(0, 1), ylim = c(0, 1))

print(p_sbc / p_coverage)

cat("\n--- Coverage summary ---\n")
cat("50% nominal coverage: empirical =",
    round(coverage_df$empirical[coverage_df$nominal == 0.50], 3), "\n")
cat("95% nominal coverage: empirical =",
    round(coverage_df$empirical[coverage_df$nominal == 0.95], 3), "\n")
```

**Reading the validation.** The SBC rank histograms for `logit_eps` and `log_n_particles` should be uniform within sampling noise. The histogram for `logit_mu` requires more care: on static Kruschke data, $\mu$ is expected to be weakly identified, so the posterior is nearly prior-like and the rank histogram should also be approximately uniform — but for a different reason. A uniform SBC histogram for $\mu$ on static data means "the posterior is as uncertain as the prior, which is exactly right given that the data contain no mutation signal." A humped histogram would mean the network is reporting false confidence; a U-shaped histogram would mean it is suppressing genuine variation. The coverage plot should track the diagonal within the grey 2-SE band for all three parameters. If the SBC histogram is U-shaped for any parameter, the posterior is overconfident — increase training data or use a more expressive flow. Both checks benefit enormously from amortization: each of the 1000 calibration checks requires only a forward pass.

### Posterior Contraction

SBC and coverage tell us the posterior is *calibrated*; they do not tell us how much the data have *informed* it. Posterior contraction quantifies this directly: for each calibration draw, compare the posterior SD to the prior SD on the unconstrained scale. A contraction near 0 means the posterior is as wide as the prior (no information gain); near 1 means the posterior has collapsed (full information).

```{r ch14_npe_contraction, eval=run_intensive_checks}
contr_file <- here("simdata", "ch14_npe_contraction.rds")

if (regenerate_simulations || !file.exists(contr_file)) {
  cat("Computing posterior contraction across calibration draws...\n")
  prior_sd_unconstrained <- c(LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_SD,
                              (log(300) - log(20)) / sqrt(12),  # uniform SD
                              LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_SD)

  # Generate a fresh, self-contained calibration set so this chunk does not
  # depend on intermediate objects from the SBC chunk above (which only exist
  # when that chunk regenerates rather than loads from cache).
  n_contr <- 200L
  set.seed(7778)
  contr_logit_eps <- rnorm(n_contr, LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_MEAN, LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_SD)
  contr_log_npart <- runif(n_contr, log(20), log(300))
  contr_logit_mu  <- rnorm(n_contr, LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_MEAN,  LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_SD)
  contr_thetas    <- cbind(contr_logit_eps, contr_log_npart, contr_logit_mu)

  contr_responses <- lapply(
    seq_len(n_contr),
    function(i) simulate_model_a(contr_thetas[i, ])
  )
  contr_y_mat <- do.call(rbind, contr_responses)

  contr_x_list <- lapply(seq_len(n_contr), function(i) {
    as.numeric(t(cbind(contr_y_mat[i, ], context_per_trial)))
  })
  contr_x_tensor <- torch$tensor(do.call(rbind, contr_x_list),
                                  dtype = torch$float32)

  contraction_rows <- vector("list", n_contr)
  for (i in seq_len(n_contr)) {
    post_samples <- npe_posterior$sample(
      sample_shape = reticulate::tuple(500L),
      x            = contr_x_tensor[i - 1L]$unsqueeze(0L)
    )$numpy()
    post_sd <- apply(post_samples, 2, sd)
    contraction_rows[[i]] <- tibble(
      draw       = i,
      parameter  = c("logit_eps", "log_n_particles", "logit_mu"),
      prior_sd   = prior_sd_unconstrained,
      post_sd    = post_sd,
      contraction = 1 - (post_sd / prior_sd_unconstrained)^2
    )
  }
  contraction_df <- bind_rows(contraction_rows)
  saveRDS(contraction_df, contr_file)
} else {
  contraction_df <- readRDS(contr_file)
}

ggplot(contraction_df, aes(x = parameter, y = contraction)) +
  geom_violin(fill = "#CC79A7", alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_jitter(alpha = 0.15, size = 0.5, width = 0.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-0.5, 1)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Posterior Contraction by Parameter (NPE, Model A)",
    subtitle = "1 = full information gain; 0 = posterior as wide as prior; <0 = something is wrong.",
    x = NULL, y = expression(1 - (sigma[post] / sigma[prior])^2)
  )

contraction_df |>
  group_by(parameter) |>
  summarise(median_contraction = round(median(contraction), 3),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  print()
```

**Reading contraction.** $\varepsilon$ should contract noticeably (median > 0.3 on a typical Kruschke run); $N_{\text{particles}}$ contracts less (the response sequence is short and only loosely informative about working-memory capacity); $\mu$ contracts very little on static data — by design. This is the quantitative version of the prior-overlay plot in Step 4: the data-information story for each parameter, measured rather than asserted.

### The Main Empirical Result: NPE vs Stan Posteriors

Here is the comparison the chapter has been building toward. We fit the same observed dataset under both Model A (via NPE) and Model B (via HMC) and overlay the two posteriors on $\varepsilon$. **The gap between them is the structural bias you accept when you replace the cognitive theory with a tractable proxy.**

```{r ch14_npe_vs_stan, eval=run_intensive_checks}
if (exists("npe_eps") && !is.null(fit_rule_stan)) {
  stan_eps <- as_draws_df(fit_rule_stan$draws("error_prob"))$error_prob

  comparison_df <- bind_rows(
    tibble(eps = npe_eps, method = "Model A (NPE, particle filter)"),
    tibble(eps = stan_eps, method = "Model B (HMC, marginalised mixture)")
  )

  ggplot(comparison_df, aes(x = eps, fill = method, color = method)) +
    geom_density(alpha = 0.4, linewidth = 0.6) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0.1, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c("Model A (NPE, particle filter)"      = "#009E73",
                                  "Model B (HMC, marginalised mixture)" = "#E69F00")) +
    scale_color_manual(values = c("Model A (NPE, particle filter)"      = "#006D4E",
                                   "Model B (HMC, marginalised mixture)" = "#B07D00")) +
    labs(
      title    = "NPE vs HMC: Posterior on ε for Model A vs Model B",
      subtitle = "Red dashed: true generating ε = 0.1\nThe gap between these distributions IS the structural bias of the proxy.",
      x = "ε", y = "Density"
    )

  cat("\n--- Posterior summaries ---\n")
  cat("Model A (NPE) posterior:    mean =", round(mean(npe_eps), 4),
      "  95% CI = [", round(quantile(npe_eps, 0.025), 4), ",",
                       round(quantile(npe_eps, 0.975), 4), "]\n")
  cat("Model B (HMC) posterior:    mean =", round(mean(stan_eps), 4),
      "  95% CI = [", round(quantile(stan_eps, 0.025), 4), ",",
                       round(quantile(stan_eps, 0.975), 4), "]\n")
  cat("True generating ε:           0.1\n")
}
```

### Demonstrating That the Gap Tracks Rule-Set Mismatch

The headline claim — "the gap is structural bias, not inference noise" — requires an experiment. We generate datasets from multiple seeds and compare the NPE-vs-Stan gap. The gap should be small when Model A converges to a rule that is in Model B's candidate set, and large when it does not.

```{r ch14_gap_demonstration}
gap_res_file <- here("simdata", "ch14_gap_demonstration_results.rds")

# 1. Handle Data Loading
if (regenerate_simulations || !file.exists(gap_res_file)) {
  if (run_intensive_checks && exists("npe_posterior") && !is.null(mod_rule_stan)) {
    cat("Generating gap demonstration data (requires Stan and NPE)...\n")
    gap_seeds <- c(42, 123, 7, 99, 55)
    
    # We use a loop and list to be much more stable on macOS than map_dfr
    gap_list <- vector("list", length(gap_seeds))
    
    for (i in seq_along(gap_seeds)) {
      s <- gap_seeds[i]
      cat("  Processing seed", s, "(", i, "of", length(gap_seeds), ")...\n")
      
      sched_g <- make_subject_schedule(stimulus_info, n_blocks, seed = s)
      sim_g   <- rule_particle_filter(
        n_particles = N_PARTICLES_FIXED, error_prob = 0.1,
        obs = as.matrix(sched_g[, c("height", "position")]),
        cat_one = sched_g$category_feedback, max_dims = MAX_RULE_DIMS, quiet = TRUE
      )

      # NPE inference (explicit types and shape debugging)
      obs_g_vec <- as.numeric(t(cbind(as.integer(sim_g$sim_response),
                                       as.matrix(sched_g[, c("height", "position")]),
                                       sched_g$category_feedback)))
      
      # Ensure matrix shape is (1, N_features)
      obs_g_mat <- matrix(obs_g_vec, nrow = 1)
      obs_g_tensor <- torch$tensor(obs_g_mat, dtype = torch$float32)
      
      # Debug print shapes once for the first seed to verify
      if (i == 1) {
        cat("  DEBUG: obs_g_tensor shape:", paste(obs_g_tensor$shape, collapse=" x "), "\n")
      }
      
      # Sample from the NPE posterior (reduced count for speed)
      n_gap_samples <- 1000L
      npe_g_samples <- npe_posterior$sample(
        sample_shape = reticulate::tuple(n_gap_samples), 
        x            = obs_g_tensor,
        show_progress_bars = FALSE
      )$numpy()
      
      npe_g_eps <- plogis(npe_g_samples[, 1])

      # Stan inference
      stan_dat_g <- list(
        ntrials = nrow(sched_g), nfeatures = 2L,
        y = as.integer(sim_g$sim_response),
        obs = as.matrix(sched_g[, c("height", "position")]),
        prior_logit_error_mean = LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_MEAN,
        prior_logit_error_sd   = LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_SD
      )
      
      fit_g <- mod_rule_stan$sample(
        data = stan_dat_g, seed = s, chains = 2, parallel_chains = 2,
        iter_warmup = 800, iter_sampling = 1000, refresh = 0, adapt_delta = 0.9
      )
      
      stan_g_eps <- as_draws_df(fit_g$draws("error_prob"))$error_prob

      gap_list[[i]] <- tibble(
        seed = s,
        npe_mean = mean(npe_g_eps), stan_mean = mean(stan_g_eps),
        gap = abs(mean(npe_g_eps) - mean(stan_g_eps)),
        npe_covers_truth  = (0.1 >= quantile(npe_g_eps, 0.025)) & (0.1 <= quantile(npe_g_eps, 0.975)),
        stan_covers_truth = (0.1 >= quantile(stan_g_eps, 0.025)) & (0.1 <= quantile(stan_g_eps, 0.975))
      )
    }
    gap_results <- bind_rows(gap_list)
    saveRDS(gap_results, gap_res_file)
  } else {
    gap_results <- NULL
  }
} else {
  gap_results <- readRDS(gap_res_file)
}

# 2. Handle Plotting
if (!is.null(gap_results)) {
  print(gap_results)

  ggplot(gap_results, aes(x = factor(seed), y = gap)) +
    geom_col(fill = "#E69F00", alpha = 0.7) +
    labs(title = "NPE-vs-Stan Gap Across Different Datasets",
         subtitle = "Large gap = converged rule NOT in Model B's set. Small gap = converged rule IS in set.",
         x = "Schedule seed", y = "|NPE mean ε − Stan mean ε|")
} else {
  cat("Gap results data not available. Set run_intensive_checks = TRUE to generate it.\n")
}
```

**Reading the gap demonstration.** If the gap varies across seeds — small for some, large for others — the structural bias is driven by rule-set mismatch, not inference noise. This is the expected outcome and the chapter's main empirical contribution.

### Amortization Payoff for Chapter 15

Because the NPE network is trained once, inference on any new subject's data takes milliseconds. For the Chapter 15 comparison across all three cognitive theories (GCM, Kalman prototype, rule particle filter), each model gets one trained NPE network, and the full multi-subject comparison runs in seconds. The code pattern:

```{r ch14_amortization_demo, eval=FALSE}
# After training, inference on N subjects is trivial:
# for each subject s:
#   x_s <- build_data_tensor(subject_responses_s, subject_schedule_s)
#   posterior_s <- npe_posterior$sample(10000L, x = x_s)
# Total time: ~N * 0.1 seconds
```

This is the methodological payoff of using NPE instead of ABC: the Chapter 15 comparison becomes both honest (fitting the cognitive theory directly) and actually executable (amortized inference scales to many subjects).


---

## Extensions to the Particle Filter

The basic filter from §"The Bayesian Particle Filter" is sufficient for the headline NPE result above. The two extensions below are optional but cognitively motivated: targeted mutation replaces the prior-sampling mutation kernel with kernels that *revise* rather than discard rules, and the scenario-aware filter exercises $\mu$'s identifiability under non-stationary feedback. Both are included here because they enable the §"NPE Parameter Recovery on Non-Stationary Data" demonstration that closes the validation chain.

### Targeted Mutation: Revision Rather Than Replacement

The `mutate_particles` function above draws each replacement rule entirely at random from the grammar — the mutated particle has no relationship to the rule it replaced. This is the simplest possible assumption: when a rule fails, discard it and start over.

Cognitively, this is implausible. The experimental literature on hypothesis testing [@levine1975cognitive] shows that people do not abandon a failing rule and sample randomly from the full hypothesis space. They **revise**: they keep the features that seem useful and adjust the part that failed. A learner using "height > 2.5" who encounters a counterexample is more likely to try "height > 3.0" or "height > 2.5 AND position < 3" than to jump to an entirely unrelated rule about position alone.

Three levels of targeted mutation are natural, in order of increasing conservatism:

**Level 1 — Perturb the threshold, keep everything else.** The rule structure (which features, which operators, which logic) is preserved; only the threshold values are resampled from a neighbourhood of their current values. This is local search in the continuous part of rule space.

**Level 2 — Keep the structure, resample one component.** The number of dimensions and the logical connective are preserved; one randomly chosen condition (feature + operator + threshold) is replaced with a freshly drawn one. This allows moderate structural revision without full randomisation.

**Level 3 — Weight features by their prevalence in high-weight particles.** Rather than replacing a particle blindly, count how often each feature appears across the top-weighted particles. Sample new rules with feature probabilities proportional to those counts. This is the most sophisticated form: the mutation kernel is informed by what the current particle population collectively "believes" is relevant.

**Level 4 — Weight rule complexity by its prevalence in high-weight particles.** A separate bias operates on the *number* of conditions rather than on which features are used. Count how many conditions (1, 2, or 3) the top-weighted particles have. Sample the dimensionality of replacement rules from that empirical distribution. If the high-weight particles are mostly 1D (e.g., "height > 2.5"), the filter generates mostly 1D replacements rather than exploring 2D conjunctions that the evidence has not yet favoured. Conversely, if 2D rules dominate, the filter concentrates its search in that part of grammar space. This is complementary to Level 3: Level 3 says *which features* to emphasise; Level 4 says *how many conditions* to use.

The four levels represent different cognitive theories:

| Level | Cognitive claim | Parameter introduced |
|---|---|---|
| Prior sampling (current) | Failed rules are abandoned; search restarts from scratch | None |
| Threshold perturbation | Learner adjusts the *boundary*, not the *dimension* | Perturbation width $\sigma_\text{perturb}$ |
| Structure revision | Learner keeps useful dimensions, revises conditions | None beyond structure |
| Feature-weighted | Learner biases search toward currently diagnostic features | None (weights are data-driven) |
| Dimensionality-weighted | Learner biases search toward the current dominant rule complexity | None (weights are data-driven) |

Each level adds a structural commitment. Levels 1–4 all require the current particle population to be inspected, and Levels 3 and 4 are most useful with a moderately large particle set where the empirical feature and dimensionality distributions are reliable estimates of what the filter collectively believes. Whether targeted mutation fits human data better than prior-sampling mutation is an open empirical question, but implementing all variants allows forward simulation of qualitatively different learner types.

```{r ch14_targeted_mutation}
# ── Level 1: Threshold perturbation ──────────────────────────────────────────
#
# Keep the rule's feature dimensions, operators, logic, and prediction.
# Resample each threshold from a Gaussian neighbourhood of its current value,
# clipped to the valid feature range. Small sigma_perturb = local search;
# large sigma_perturb approaches prior-sampling mutation.
#
# sigma_perturb : standard deviation of the Gaussian perturbation on the
#                 threshold scale. Default 0.5 is roughly 10% of a [0,5] range.

mutate_threshold <- function(rule, feature_range, sigma_perturb = 0.5) {
  new_rule <- rule
  for (k in seq_along(rule$thresholds)) {
    dim_k  <- rule$dimensions[k]
    lo     <- feature_range[[dim_k]][1]
    hi     <- feature_range[[dim_k]][2]
    # Perturb threshold, then clip to valid range so the rule stays meaningful.
    new_thresh <- rule$thresholds[k] + rnorm(1, 0, sigma_perturb)
    new_rule$thresholds[k] <- pmax(lo, pmin(hi, new_thresh))
  }
  new_rule
}

# ── Level 2: Single-component revision ───────────────────────────────────────
#
# Preserve the number of dimensions and the logical connective.
# Randomly select one condition slot and replace its (feature, operator,
# threshold) with a fresh draw — leaving all other conditions unchanged.
# For a 1D rule this is equivalent to replacing the single condition, so it
# reduces to a constrained version of prior-sampling mutation.

mutate_one_condition <- function(rule, n_features, feature_range) {
  new_rule  <- rule
  n_conds   <- length(rule$dimensions)
  k         <- sample.int(n_conds, 1)          # condition slot to revise
  new_dim   <- sample.int(n_features, 1)       # new feature for that slot
  new_op    <- sample(c(">", "<"), 1)
  new_thresh <- runif(1, feature_range[[new_dim]][1], feature_range[[new_dim]][2])

  new_rule$dimensions[k]  <- new_dim
  new_rule$operations[k]  <- new_op
  new_rule$thresholds[k]  <- new_thresh
  new_rule
}

# ── Level 3: Feature-weighted mutation ───────────────────────────────────────
#
# Count how often each feature dimension appears across the top-weighted
# particles. Sample new rules using those counts as feature probabilities,
# so dimensions that are currently "popular" among high-weight hypotheses
# are more likely to appear in the replacement rule.
#
# This does not change the rule grammar — it only biases which features
# are sampled. The generated rule is structurally identical to what
# generate_random_rule produces, but with a non-uniform feature distribution.
#
# particles  : list of rule objects (the full particle set)
# weights    : current normalised weight vector
# top_frac   : fraction of particles treated as "high-weight" for counting
#              (default 0.25 = top quartile)

feature_weights_from_particles <- function(particles, weights,
                                           n_features, top_frac = 0.25) {
  n        <- length(particles)
  top_n    <- max(1L, round(n * top_frac))
  top_idx  <- order(weights, decreasing = TRUE)[seq_len(top_n)]

  # Count feature appearances across top particles.
  counts <- tabulate(
    unlist(lapply(particles[top_idx], `[[`, "dimensions")),
    nbins = n_features
  )
  # Add a small floor so no feature has zero probability (the grammar
  # allows any feature in any position, so zero probability is too strong).
  probs <- (counts + 0.5) / sum(counts + 0.5)
  probs
}

generate_rule_with_feature_probs <- function(n_features, max_dims,
                                              feature_range, feature_probs,
                                              nested_prob = 0.25) {
  # Same structure as generate_random_rule but samples dimensions according
  # to feature_probs rather than uniformly.
  use_nested <- (n_features >= 2) && (runif(1) < nested_prob)

  sample_thresh <- function(di) runif(1, feature_range[[di]][1],
                                         feature_range[[di]][2])
  sample_dim    <- function() sample.int(n_features, 1, prob = feature_probs)

  if (use_nested) {
    dims_used  <- replicate(3, sample_dim())
    thresholds <- mapply(sample_thresh, dims_used)
    operations <- sample(c(">", "<"), 3, replace = TRUE)
    list(
      dimensions         = dims_used,
      thresholds         = thresholds,
      operations         = operations,
      outer_logic        = sample(c("AND", "OR"), 1),
      inner_logic        = sample(c("AND", "OR"), 1),
      prediction_if_true = sample(0:1, 1)
    )
  } else {
    num_dims   <- sample(seq_len(min(max_dims, n_features)), 1)
    dims_used  <- sort(replicate(num_dims, sample_dim()))
    thresholds <- mapply(sample_thresh, dims_used)
    operations <- sample(c(">", "<"), num_dims, replace = TRUE)
    list(
      dimensions         = dims_used,
      thresholds         = thresholds,
      operations         = operations,
      logic              = if (num_dims == 2) sample(c("AND", "OR"), 1) else NA,
      prediction_if_true = sample(0:1, 1)
    )
  }
}

# ── Level 4: Dimensionality-weighted mutation ─────────────────────────────────
#
# Count how many conditions (1, 2, or 3) the top-weighted particles use.
# Sample the number of conditions in the replacement rule from that empirical
# distribution. Features within the chosen dimensionality are still drawn
# uniformly from the grammar.
#
# This is orthogonal to Level 3: Level 3 biases *which features* appear;
# Level 4 biases *how many conditions* the replacement rule has.

dim_weights_from_particles <- function(particles, weights,
                                        max_dims, top_frac = 0.25) {
  n       <- length(particles)
  top_n   <- max(1L, round(n * top_frac))
  top_idx <- order(weights, decreasing = TRUE)[seq_len(top_n)]

  ndims  <- sapply(particles[top_idx], function(r) length(r$dimensions))
  counts <- tabulate(ndims, nbins = max_dims)
  (counts + 0.5) / sum(counts + 0.5)   # Laplace floor: no dimensionality excluded
}

generate_rule_with_dim_probs <- function(n_features, max_dims,
                                          feature_range, dim_probs) {
  num_dims   <- sample(seq_len(min(max_dims, n_features)), 1, prob = dim_probs)
  use_nested <- (num_dims == 3 && n_features >= 2)

  sample_thresh <- function(di) runif(1, feature_range[[di]][1],
                                         feature_range[[di]][2])

  if (use_nested) {
    dims_used  <- sample.int(n_features, 3, replace = TRUE)
    thresholds <- mapply(sample_thresh, dims_used)
    operations <- sample(c(">", "<"), 3, replace = TRUE)
    list(
      dimensions         = dims_used,
      thresholds         = thresholds,
      operations         = operations,
      outer_logic        = sample(c("AND", "OR"), 1),
      inner_logic        = sample(c("AND", "OR"), 1),
      prediction_if_true = sample(0:1, 1)
    )
  } else {
    dims_used  <- sort(sample.int(n_features, num_dims, replace = TRUE))
    thresholds <- mapply(sample_thresh, dims_used)
    operations <- sample(c(">", "<"), num_dims, replace = TRUE)
    list(
      dimensions         = dims_used,
      thresholds         = thresholds,
      operations         = operations,
      logic              = if (num_dims == 2) sample(c("AND", "OR"), 1) else NA,
      prediction_if_true = sample(0:1, 1)
    )
  }
}

# ── Unified targeted mutation driver ─────────────────────────────────────────
#
# Replaces mutate_particles() for forward simulation of targeted learners.
# mutation_type controls which cognitive theory is applied:
#   "prior"            — original prior-sampling mutation (no revision)
#   "threshold"        — Level 1: perturb thresholds, keep structure
#   "component"        — Level 2: replace one condition, keep the rest
#   "feature_weighted" — Level 3: resample with feature-prevalence bias
#   "dim_weighted"     — Level 4: resample with dimensionality-prevalence bias
#
# All types share the same mutation_prob parameter: probability per particle
# per trial of being mutated at all. The targeted types differ only in
# *how* the replacement rule is generated.

mutate_particles_targeted <- function(particles, weights,
                                       mutation_prob,
                                       n_features, max_dims, feature_range,
                                       mutation_type   = c("prior",
                                                           "threshold",
                                                           "component",
                                                           "feature_weighted",
                                                           "dim_weighted"),
                                       sigma_perturb   = 0.5,
                                       top_frac        = 0.25) {
  mutation_type <- match.arg(mutation_type)
  if (mutation_prob <= 0) return(particles)

  n          <- length(particles)
  to_mutate  <- which(runif(n) < mutation_prob)
  if (length(to_mutate) == 0) return(particles)

  # Compute population-level statistics once per call from high-weight particles.
  feat_probs <- if (mutation_type == "feature_weighted")
    feature_weights_from_particles(particles, weights, n_features, top_frac)
  else NULL

  dim_probs <- if (mutation_type == "dim_weighted")
    dim_weights_from_particles(particles, weights, max_dims, top_frac)
  else NULL

  for (i in to_mutate) {
    particles[[i]] <- switch(mutation_type,
      prior = generate_random_rule(n_features, max_dims, feature_range),

      threshold = mutate_threshold(particles[[i]], feature_range, sigma_perturb),

      component = mutate_one_condition(particles[[i]], n_features, feature_range),

      feature_weighted = generate_rule_with_feature_probs(
        n_features, max_dims, feature_range, feat_probs
      ),

      dim_weighted = generate_rule_with_dim_probs(
        n_features, max_dims, feature_range, dim_probs
      )
    )
  }
  particles
}
```

**Key design notes:**

* `mutate_particles_targeted` is a drop-in replacement for `mutate_particles` in any particle filter that wants to explore targeted revision. It takes the same `mutation_prob` argument and adds `mutation_type`, so existing code that passes `mutation_type = "prior"` is identical to the original.
* The perturbation width `sigma_perturb` in Level 1 is a new structural commitment. Small values (< 0.2) produce almost-local search; large values (> 2.0) approach prior-sampling. It is not estimated from data in Model A — it would need to be either fixed by cognitive argument or added to the NPE prior.
* Levels 3 and 4 (`feature_weighted`, `dim_weighted`) both depend on the empirical distribution of the top-weighted particles and are most reliable with a moderately large particle set. A Laplace floor of 0.5 prevents any feature or dimensionality from being completely excluded. The two can in principle be combined — biasing both *which features* and *how many conditions* simultaneously — by composing `feature_weights_from_particles` and `dim_weights_from_particles` inside a single generator.
* All four targeted variants are more conservative than prior-sampling: they keep more of the current rule intact, which means they explore less of the rule space per unit time. On a task where the true rule is very different from anything currently in the particle set, targeted mutation may be slower to recover than prior-sampling.

### Scenario-Aware Particle Filter

We extend `rule_particle_filter` to take a `mutation_prob` argument and a `scenario` switch that controls how the true feedback sequence is produced. The scenarios follow the canonical framework from Chapter 12: static Kruschke, performance-contingent abrupt shifts, and smooth drift (in that order). Note that contingent-shift flips are endogenous to the agent's own accuracy — a noisier agent triggers fewer flips and accumulates less information about $\mu$ (see Chapter 12 for discussion).

```{r ch14_drift_trajectory}
make_drift_trajectory <- function(ntrials, drift_sigma = 0.05, seed = 1) {
  set.seed(seed)
  mu0_init <- c(1.75, 3.25)
  mu1_init <- c(3.25, 1.75)
  d0 <- apply(matrix(rnorm(ntrials * 2, 0, drift_sigma), ncol = 2), 2, cumsum)
  d1 <- apply(matrix(rnorm(ntrials * 2, 0, drift_sigma), ncol = 2), 2, cumsum)
  list(
    mu0 = sweep(d0, 2, mu0_init, "+"),
    mu1 = sweep(d1, 2, mu1_init, "+")
  )
}
```

```{r ch14_scenario_particle_filter}
rule_particle_filter_scenario <- function(schedule,
                                          error_prob,
                                          mutation_prob              = 0.0,
                                          mutation_type              = "prior",
                                          sigma_perturb              = 0.5,
                                          n_particles                = N_PARTICLES_FIXED,
                                          max_dims                   = MAX_RULE_DIMS,
                                          resample_threshold_factor  = RESAMPLE_THRESHOLD,
                                          scenario                   = c("static",
                                                                          "contingent_shift",
                                                                          "drift"),
                                          streak_target              = 8,
                                          drift_traj                 = NULL,
                                          quiet                      = TRUE) {
  scenario   <- match.arg(scenario)
  n_trials   <- nrow(schedule)
  obs        <- as.matrix(schedule[, c("height", "position")])
  base_lab   <- schedule$category_feedback
  n_features <- ncol(obs)
  fr         <- lapply(seq_len(n_features), function(d) range(obs[, d]))

  ps        <- initialize_particles(n_particles, n_features, max_dims, fr)
  particles <- ps$particles
  weights   <- ps$weights

  label_flip         <- FALSE
  streak             <- 0L

  response_probs     <- numeric(n_trials)
  sim_response       <- integer(n_trials)
  observed_feedback  <- integer(n_trials)
  label_flip_trace   <- logical(n_trials)

  for (i in seq_len(n_trials)) {
    stim <- as.numeric(obs[i, ])

    # ── Prediction ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    preds        <- sapply(particles, evaluate_rule, stimulus = stim)
    p_cat1_rules <- ifelse(preds == 1, 1 - error_prob, error_prob)
    p            <- sum(weights * p_cat1_rules)
    p            <- pmax(1e-9, pmin(1 - 1e-9, p))
    response_probs[i] <- p
    sim_response[i]   <- rbinom(1, 1, p)

    # ── Observed feedback depends on scenario ─────────────────────────
    fb <- switch(scenario,
      static           = base_lab[i],
      contingent_shift = if (label_flip) 1L - base_lab[i] else base_lab[i],
      drift            = {
        d0 <- sum(abs(stim - drift_traj$mu0[i, ]))
        d1 <- sum(abs(stim - drift_traj$mu1[i, ]))
        as.integer(d1 < d0)
      }
    )
    observed_feedback[i] <- fb
    label_flip_trace[i]  <- label_flip

    if (scenario == "contingent_shift") {
      streak <- if (sim_response[i] == fb) streak + 1L else 0L
      if (streak >= streak_target) { label_flip <- !label_flip; streak <- 0L }
    }

    # ── Weight update ────────────────────────────────────────────────
    weights <- update_weights(particles, weights, stim, fb, error_prob)

    # ── Resample if ESS is low ───────────────────────────────────────
    ess <- 1 / sum(weights^2)
    if (ess < n_particles * resample_threshold_factor) {
      rs        <- resample_particles(particles, weights)
      particles <- rs$particles
      weights   <- rs$weights
    }

    # ── Mutation (after resampling) ───────────────────────────────────
    # mutation_type controls whether replacement rules are drawn from the
    # prior ("prior") or generated via one of the targeted revision
    # strategies ("threshold", "component", "feature_weighted", "dim_weighted").
    particles <- mutate_particles_targeted(particles, weights,
                                           mutation_prob, n_features,
                                           max_dims, fr,
                                           mutation_type = mutation_type,
                                           sigma_perturb = sigma_perturb)
  }

  schedule |>
    mutate(
      prob_cat1         = response_probs,
      sim_response      = sim_response,
      observed_feedback = observed_feedback,
      label_flip        = label_flip_trace,
      correct           = as.integer(sim_response == observed_feedback)
    )
}
```

### Agent Wrapper

```{r ch14_scenario_agent_wrapper}
simulate_rule_agent_scenario <- function(agent_id, scenario,
                                         error_prob, mutation_prob,
                                         subject_seed,
                                         mutation_type = "prior",
                                         sigma_perturb = 0.5,
                                         n_particles   = N_PARTICLES_FIXED,
                                         max_dims      = MAX_RULE_DIMS,
                                         streak_target = 8,
                                         drift_sigma   = 0.05) {
  schedule <- make_subject_schedule(stimulus_info, n_blocks, seed = subject_seed)
  drift_tr <- if (scenario == "drift")
    make_drift_trajectory(nrow(schedule), drift_sigma, seed = subject_seed)
  else NULL

  out <- rule_particle_filter_scenario(
    schedule                  = schedule,
    error_prob                = error_prob,
    mutation_prob             = mutation_prob,
    mutation_type             = mutation_type,
    sigma_perturb             = sigma_perturb,
    n_particles               = n_particles,
    max_dims                  = max_dims,
    scenario                  = scenario,
    streak_target             = streak_target,
    drift_traj                = drift_tr,
    resample_threshold_factor = RESAMPLE_THRESHOLD
  )

  out |>
    mutate(
      agent_id           = agent_id,
      scenario           = scenario,
      error_prob_true    = error_prob,
      mutation_prob_true = mutation_prob,
      mutation_type      = mutation_type
    ) |>
    group_by(agent_id) |>
    mutate(cumulative_accuracy = cumsum(correct) / row_number()) |>
    ungroup()
}
```

::: {.callout-note title="Unified scenario framework across the three chapters"}
All three chapters use the same three scenarios in the same order (static → contingent shift → drift), reflecting **increasing assumption match** between the scenario's data-generating process and the model's structural commitments. The canonical definition and rationale are in Chapter 12. What differs across chapters is *how well* the assumption-matched scenario actually matches:

| Chapter | Model | Contingent shift | Smooth drift |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ch. 11 — Decay GCM | Exemplar memory with forgetting | Misspecified (no abrupt mechanism); strong $\lambda$ signal via post-flip recovery | Graceful degradation; approximate match |
| Ch. 12 — Kalman prototype | Gaussian-drift prototype tracker | Misspecified; filter smooths the reversal | **Exact match**: Gaussian drift is the model's native story |
| Ch. 13 — Rule particle filter | Bayesian particle filter + mutation | Approximate; mutation lets the filter recover | Approximate; mutation supplies fresh candidates but does not track direction |

Readers should compare the learning-curve panels across chapters to see how each model's structural commitments show up in its sensitivity to the two non-stationary scenarios. The Kalman prototype is uniquely well-matched to smooth drift; the decay GCM and the rule filter are both general-purpose in ways that make them approximately suitable for either non-stationary scenario, for different mechanistic reasons.
:::

### Visualising the Three Scenarios

We simulate 8 agents per cell across a 3 × 3 grid of scenarios × mutation rates, at fixed $\varepsilon = 0.1$ and $N_{\text{particles}} = 100$. As in Chapters 11 and 12, we expect mutation to *hurt* on static data (it purges rules that already match) and *help* on non-stationary data (it injects fresh candidates the filter can reweight when the environment changes).

```{r ch14_scenario_viz}
rule_scenario_viz_file <- here("simdata", "ch14_rule_scenario_viz.csv")

if (regenerate_simulations || !file.exists(rule_scenario_viz_file)) {
  viz_grid <- expand_grid(
    scenario      = c("static", "contingent_shift", "drift"),
    mutation_prob = c(0.0, 0.05, 0.2),
    agent_id      = 1:8
  ) |>
    mutate(subject_seed = 50000 + row_number())

  rule_viz_sim <- pmap_dfr(
    list(agent_id      = viz_grid$agent_id,
         scenario      = viz_grid$scenario,
         mutation_prob = viz_grid$mutation_prob,
         subject_seed  = viz_grid$subject_seed),
    function(agent_id, scenario, mutation_prob, subject_seed) {
      simulate_rule_agent_scenario(
        agent_id      = agent_id,
        scenario      = scenario,
        error_prob    = 0.1,
        mutation_prob = mutation_prob,
        subject_seed  = subject_seed
      )
    }
  )
  write_csv(rule_viz_sim, rule_scenario_viz_file)
} else {
  rule_viz_sim <- read_csv(rule_scenario_viz_file, show_col_types = FALSE)
}

ggplot(rule_viz_sim,
       aes(x = trial_within_subject, y = cumulative_accuracy,
           color = factor(mutation_prob_true))) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "line",
               aes(group = factor(mutation_prob_true)), linewidth = 1.1) +
  stat_summary(fun.data = mean_se, geom = "ribbon", alpha = 0.15,
               aes(fill = factor(mutation_prob_true),
                   group = factor(mutation_prob_true)),
               color = NA) +
  facet_wrap(~scenario, ncol = 3,
             labeller = as_labeller(c(
               static           = "Static Kruschke",
               contingent_shift = "Performance-contingent flips",
               drift            = "Smooth drift"))) +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "plasma", name = expression(mu)) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option  = "plasma", name = expression(mu)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  labs(
    title    = "Rule Particle Filter Learning Curves Across Scenarios",
    subtitle = paste0("Mean ± SE across 8 agents per cell; ε = 0.1, N_particles = 100"),
    x = "Trial", y = "Cumulative accuracy"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

**Reading the panels.** On static data, $\mu = 0$ wins: the filter can converge on the right rule and keep it. Higher $\mu$ trades accuracy for adaptability — with 5% mutation per trial per particle, there is always ~5% of the particle cloud freshly drawn from the prior, which cannot (yet) have high weight. On contingent-shift data, the curves sort in the opposite direction after the first flip: $\mu = 0$ collapses after the reversal and never recovers, while $\mu > 0$ produces the characteristic sawtooth recovery. On drift data, moderate $\mu$ tracks the drifting boundary better than $\mu = 0$.

### Assumption Match Across Scenarios

| Scenario | What "changes" in the world | What mutation assumes | Match |
|---|---|---|---|
| Static | Nothing | Stochastic rule turnover at rate $\mu$ | **Harmless at $\mu = 0$**; costly for $\mu > 0$ |
| Contingent shift | Abrupt label reversals | Stochastic rule turnover | **Approximate**: mutation lets the filter recover, but there is no mechanism for abrupt, synchronised re-initialisation |
| Smooth drift | Gaussian drift of category centres | Stochastic rule turnover | **Approximate**: mutation supplies fresh candidates but is not literally tracking the drift direction |

Unlike the Kalman prototype's $q$, mutation is not a *model* of the drift process — it is a coarse rejuvenation mechanism that keeps the hypothesis space alive so the filter can react to whatever changes the environment throws at it. The matching story in Chapter 13 was "smooth drift fits the model's generative assumption exactly"; here it is "mutation is a general-purpose fix that happens to suffice in both non-stationary scenarios." This is the cognitive-theory contrast: rule-based learners have no commitment to *how* the environment changes, only that their hypotheses need to stay fresh.

### How This Strengthens the Model A vs Model B Gap

The Stan mixture (Model B) is built on a fixed candidate rule set enumerated before inference begins. There is no analogue to $\mu$ — mixture weights can reassign posterior mass across the candidate set, but nothing in Model B *creates* rules that were not in the candidate set. On static data this is an acceptable approximation as long as the true rule is in the candidate set (Ch. 13's §"Discharging the Post-Hoc Rule Selection" argues exactly this). On contingent-shift or drift data, the candidate set that correctly covered the initial environment will not cover the post-shift environment, and Model B has no mechanism to repair itself.

Concretely: if we take the contingent-shift simulated data from the plot above and fit Model B to it, Model B's posterior predictive learning curve will be stuck at the pre-shift accuracy plateau — it has no way to produce the sawtooth. The same fit under NPE (with $\varepsilon$ and $\mu$ both inferred) can match the data because Model A *is* the generative process. **The gap between Model A and Model B on non-stationary data is therefore not a subtle bias — it is a structural ceiling.** This is the sharpened version of the argument in §"The Main Empirical Result": the cost of replacing an intractable cognitive theory with a tractable proxy is bounded when the task is stationary, and unbounded when it is not.

### NPE Parameter Recovery on Non-Stationary Data

The NPE network in §"Neural Posterior Estimation for Model A" was trained with a fixed schedule and a static feedback sequence. The forward simulations above showed that $\mu$ produces a distinctive sawtooth signature on contingent-shift data and a steady-tracking signature on drift data — signatures that are absent on static data. The substantive prediction is therefore: **the same trained NPE network should return a $\mu$ posterior that is prior-like on static data, and sharply contracted on the two non-stationary scenarios.**

We test that prediction directly. We simulate one observed dataset per scenario at a moderate true mutation rate ($\mu = 0.1$, $\varepsilon = 0.1$, $N = 100$), feed each through the trained network, and overlay the three resulting $\mu$ posteriors against the prior.

```{r ch14_npe_param_recovery_nonstationary, eval=python_available}
recovery_file <- here("simdata", "ch14_npe_param_recovery_nonstationary.rds")

if (regenerate_simulations || !file.exists(recovery_file)) {
  if (run_intensive_checks) {
    set.seed(2027)
    rec_scenarios <- c("static", "contingent_shift", "drift")
    rec_eps   <- 0.1
    rec_mu    <- 0.1
    rec_N     <- 100L

    rec_list <- vector("list", length(rec_scenarios))
    for (s in seq_along(rec_scenarios)) {
      sc        <- rec_scenarios[s]
      drift_tr  <- if (sc == "drift")
        make_drift_trajectory(nrow(npe_schedule), drift_sigma = 0.05, seed = 2027)
      else NULL

      sim_s <- rule_particle_filter_scenario(
        schedule                  = npe_schedule,
        error_prob                = rec_eps,
        mutation_prob             = rec_mu,
        n_particles               = rec_N,
        max_dims                  = MAX_RULE_DIMS,
        scenario                  = sc,
        drift_traj                = drift_tr
      )

      x_vec <- as.numeric(t(cbind(as.integer(sim_s$sim_response),
                                   npe_obs_mat,
                                   sim_s$observed_feedback)))
      x_tens <- torch$tensor(matrix(x_vec, nrow = 1), dtype = torch$float32)

      post_s <- npe_posterior$sample(
        sample_shape       = reticulate::tuple(2000L),
        x                  = x_tens,
        show_progress_bars = FALSE
      )$numpy()

      rec_list[[s]] <- tibble(
        scenario = sc,
        eps      = plogis(post_s[, 1]),
        n_part   = exp(post_s[, 2]),
        mu       = plogis(post_s[, 3])
      )
    }
    recovery_df <- bind_rows(rec_list)
    saveRDS(recovery_df, recovery_file)
  } else {
    recovery_df <- NULL
  }
} else {
  recovery_df <- readRDS(recovery_file)
}

if (!is.null(recovery_df)) {
  prior_mu_grid <- plogis(rnorm(2000, LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_MEAN, LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_SD))

  ggplot(recovery_df, aes(x = mu, fill = scenario, color = scenario)) +
    geom_density(alpha = 0.4, linewidth = 0.7) +
    geom_density(data = tibble(mu = prior_mu_grid),
                 aes(x = mu),
                 inherit.aes = FALSE,
                 linewidth = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey30") +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0.1, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
    scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "plasma") +
    scale_color_viridis_d(option = "plasma") +
    labs(
      title    = "NPE μ Posterior Across Scenarios (Same Trained Network)",
      subtitle = "Dashed grey = prior. Red dashed = true generating μ = 0.1.\nμ should contract on contingent_shift and drift; remain prior-like on static.",
      x = "μ", y = "Density"
    )

  recovery_df |>
    group_by(scenario) |>
    summarise(
      mu_mean   = round(mean(mu), 3),
      mu_q025   = round(quantile(mu, 0.025), 3),
      mu_q975   = round(quantile(mu, 0.975), 3),
      mu_sd     = round(sd(mu), 3),
      .groups   = "drop"
    ) |>
    print()
}
```

**Reading the demonstration.** The static panel should reproduce the Step 4 result: a $\mu$ posterior nearly identical to the dashed prior, because the data carry no information about mutation rate. The contingent-shift and drift panels should both show contracted, off-prior $\mu$ posteriors centred on (or near) the true 0.1 — the sawtooth and tracking signatures are exactly the regularities that render $\mu$ identifiable. This is the parameter-recovery counterpart of the SBC + expected-coverage block above: SBC tells us the network is calibrated; this experiment tells us *when* the data are informative enough for that calibration to translate into useful contraction.

The same trained network handles all three scenarios because the embedding is conditioned on the per-trial feedback sequence — a contingent-shift dataset and a static dataset are simply different points in the input space. Retraining is unnecessary as long as the schedule is preserved.

### Fitting One NPE Agent per Scenario

The proof-of-concept above compared $\mu$ posteriors across scenarios on a single dataset each. We now make this systematic. We fit one representative agent — true $\varepsilon = 0.1$, $\mu = 0.1$, $N = 100$ — per scenario and overlay all three NPE posteriors against the prior for each parameter. The same trained network is used throughout.

```{r ch14_npe_fit_per_scenario, eval=python_available}
npe_scenario_fits_file <- here("simdata", "ch14_npe_scenario_fits.rds")

if (regenerate_simulations || !file.exists(npe_scenario_fits_file)) {
  if (run_intensive_checks) {
    set.seed(4242)
    fit_eps   <- 0.1
    fit_mu    <- 0.1
    fit_npart <- 100L

    scenarios_to_fit <- c("static", "contingent_shift", "drift")
    fit_draws_list <- vector("list", length(scenarios_to_fit))

    for (s in seq_along(scenarios_to_fit)) {
      sc       <- scenarios_to_fit[s]
      drift_tr <- if (sc == "drift")
        make_drift_trajectory(nrow(npe_schedule), drift_sigma = 0.05, seed = 4242)
      else NULL

      sim_s <- rule_particle_filter_scenario(
        schedule                  = npe_schedule,
        error_prob                = fit_eps,
        mutation_prob             = fit_mu,
        n_particles               = fit_npart,
        max_dims                  = MAX_RULE_DIMS,
        scenario                  = sc,
        drift_traj                = drift_tr
      )

      x_vec  <- as.numeric(t(cbind(as.integer(sim_s$sim_response),
                                   npe_obs_mat,
                                   sim_s$observed_feedback)))
      x_tens <- torch$tensor(matrix(x_vec, nrow = 1), dtype = torch$float32)

      post <- npe_posterior$sample(
        sample_shape       = reticulate::tuple(3000L),
        x                  = x_tens,
        show_progress_bars = FALSE
      )$numpy()

      fit_draws_list[[s]] <- tibble(
        scenario = sc,
        eps      = plogis(post[, 1]),
        n_part   = exp(post[, 2]),
        mu       = plogis(post[, 3])
      )
    }

    npe_scenario_fits <- bind_rows(fit_draws_list)
    saveRDS(npe_scenario_fits, npe_scenario_fits_file)
    cat("Scenario fits computed and saved.\n")
  } else {
    npe_scenario_fits <- NULL
  }
} else {
  npe_scenario_fits <- readRDS(npe_scenario_fits_file)
  cat("Scenario fits loaded.\n")
}

if (!is.null(npe_scenario_fits)) {
  n_draws_prior <- 3000L
  prior_df <- tibble(
    eps    = plogis(rnorm(n_draws_prior, LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_MEAN, LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_SD)),
    mu     = plogis(rnorm(n_draws_prior, LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_MEAN, LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_SD)),
    n_part = exp(runif(n_draws_prior, log(20), log(300)))
  )

  scenario_labels <- c(
    static           = "Static Kruschke",
    contingent_shift = "Contingent shift",
    drift            = "Smooth drift"
  )

  # ── ε panels ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  p_eps_scen <- ggplot(npe_scenario_fits,
                       aes(x = eps, fill = scenario, color = scenario)) +
    geom_density(alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 0.7) +
    geom_density(data = prior_df, aes(x = eps),
                 inherit.aes = FALSE, linetype = "dashed",
                 color = "grey30", linewidth = 0.5) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0.1, color = "red", linetype = "dotted") +
    scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "plasma",
                         labels = scenario_labels,
                         name   = "Scenario") +
    scale_color_viridis_d(option = "plasma",
                          labels = scenario_labels,
                          name   = "Scenario") +
    labs(title    = "NPE Posterior for ε Across Scenarios",
         subtitle = "Grey dashed = prior; red dotted = true ε = 0.1",
         x = "ε", y = "Density") +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom")

  # ── μ panels ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  p_mu_scen <- ggplot(npe_scenario_fits,
                      aes(x = mu, fill = scenario, color = scenario)) +
    geom_density(alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 0.7) +
    geom_density(data = prior_df, aes(x = mu),
                 inherit.aes = FALSE, linetype = "dashed",
                 color = "grey30", linewidth = 0.5) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0.1, color = "red", linetype = "dotted") +
    scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "plasma",
                         labels = scenario_labels,
                         name   = "Scenario") +
    scale_color_viridis_d(option = "plasma",
                          labels = scenario_labels,
                          name   = "Scenario") +
    labs(title    = "NPE Posterior for μ Across Scenarios",
         subtitle = "Grey dashed = prior; red dotted = true μ = 0.1\nμ contracts on non-stationary data; remains prior-like on static",
         x = "μ", y = "Density") +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom")

  print(p_eps_scen / p_mu_scen)
}
```

**Reading the posteriors.** $\varepsilon$ should be well-concentrated near 0.1 in all three scenarios — any reasonably long response sequence provides information about the agent's error rate regardless of the environment's dynamics. $\mu$ should show the opposite pattern: the static posterior should be nearly indistinguishable from the prior (grey dashed), while the contingent-shift and drift posteriors should be noticeably narrower and centred closer to the true value of 0.1. The separation between the three $\mu$ posteriors is the visual argument for the identifiability claim made throughout this section.

### Prior–Posterior Update Across Scenarios (NPE)

We render the prior–posterior contrast explicitly, one column per scenario. This mirrors the prior–posterior update plots in the single-agent sections of Chapters 11 and 12, now extended to the three-scenario setting via NPE rather than MCMC.

```{r ch14_npe_prior_post_scenarios, eval=python_available}
if (!is.null(npe_scenario_fits)) {
  n_draws_prior <- 3000L
  prior_eps_draws <- plogis(rnorm(n_draws_prior,
                                  LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_MEAN, LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_SD))
  prior_mu_draws  <- plogis(rnorm(n_draws_prior,
                                  LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_MEAN,  LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_SD))

  make_prior_post_panel <- function(prior_vec, posterior_vec,
                                    scenario_label, param_name,
                                    true_val, xlab) {
    df_post  <- tibble(value = posterior_vec, type = "Posterior")
    df_prior <- tibble(value = prior_vec,     type = "Prior")
    df_all   <- bind_rows(df_prior, df_post)

    ggplot(df_all, aes(x = value, fill = type, color = type)) +
      geom_density(alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 0.7) +
      geom_vline(xintercept = true_val, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
      scale_fill_manual(values  = c("Prior" = "grey60", "Posterior" = "#0072B2")) +
      scale_color_manual(values = c("Prior" = "grey40", "Posterior" = "#0072B2")) +
      labs(title = scenario_label,
           x     = xlab, y = "Density") +
      theme(legend.position = "none",
            plot.title = element_text(size = 10))
  }

  sc_list <- list(
    list(label = "Static Kruschke",      key = "static"),
    list(label = "Contingent shift",     key = "contingent_shift"),
    list(label = "Smooth drift",         key = "drift")
  )

  # Row 1: ε panels
  eps_panels <- lapply(sc_list, function(sc) {
    post_sc <- npe_scenario_fits |> dplyr::filter(scenario == sc$key)
    make_prior_post_panel(prior_eps_draws, post_sc$eps,
                          sc$label, "epsilon", 0.1, "ε")
  })

  # Row 2: μ panels
  mu_panels <- lapply(sc_list, function(sc) {
    post_sc <- npe_scenario_fits |> dplyr::filter(scenario == sc$key)
    make_prior_post_panel(prior_mu_draws, post_sc$mu,
                          sc$label, "mu", 0.1, "μ")
  })

  # Compose
  top_row    <- (eps_panels[[1]] | eps_panels[[2]] | eps_panels[[3]])
  bottom_row <- (mu_panels[[1]]  | mu_panels[[2]]  | mu_panels[[3]])

  print(
    top_row / bottom_row +
      plot_annotation(
        title    = "Prior → Posterior Updates Across Scenarios (NPE)",
        subtitle = "Row 1: ε  |  Row 2: μ  |  Blue = posterior, grey = prior, red dashed = truth (0.1)",
        theme    = theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))
      )
  )
}
```

**Reading the grid.** The top row ($\varepsilon$) should look similar across all three columns — the three posteriors should all be narrower than the prior and centred near 0.1. The bottom row ($\mu$) is where the story is: the static column should show minimal updating (posterior ≈ prior), while the contingent-shift and drift columns should show noticeable posterior narrowing. The magnitude of the update in the shift and drift panels is the NPE equivalent of what the SBC rank histograms and coverage plots formalise below.

### One-Shot Parameter Recovery Across Scenarios (NPE)

We now sweep a grid of true parameter values to assess whether the NPE network recovers $\varepsilon$ and $\mu$ systematically across the plausible range. We use three values of each parameter crossed with three scenarios, with three independent realisations per cell (81 NPE inferences total). Because NPE inference is amortised — each forward pass costs milliseconds — this sweep is computationally trivial compared to the equivalent MCMC sweep in Chapters 11 and 12.

